title: Building a Credit History
description: Every time you use a credit card and repay it, that activity is reported to credit bureaus like CIBIL, building a track record of how reliably you handle borrowed money. This history is what lenders look at when you apply for bigger loans later, like a home or car loan.
whoBenefitsMost: First-time borrowers and young professionals who don't yet have a loan history and want to establish one before they need a larger loan.
thingsToRemember: A credit history only helps if it's a good one — consistent late payments or high balances can hurt your score more than having no credit history at all.

title: Emergency Purchasing Power
description: A credit card gives you access to funds for genuine emergencies — a medical bill, urgent travel, or a sudden repair — without needing cash on hand or having to liquidate savings on short notice.
whoBenefitsMost: Anyone who doesn't have a large emergency fund yet, or wants a backup option in addition to savings.
thingsToRemember: This is meant for occasional true emergencies, not routine shortfalls. If you're regularly relying on your card to get through the month, that's a sign to review your budget, not a normal use of the card.

title: Cashback and Rewards
description: Many cards return a small percentage of your spending as cashback, points, or miles, effectively giving a discount on purchases you were already planning to make.
whoBenefitsMost: People with predictable, recurring monthly expenses (groceries, bills, fuel) who pay their balance in full and can capture rewards without paying interest.
thingsToRemember: Rewards are only a genuine benefit if you weren't going to overspend to earn them, and if you pay off the bill in full — interest charges almost always outweigh the rewards earned.

title: Airport Lounge Access
description: Some cards offer complimentary or discounted access to airport lounges, providing a more comfortable space to wait during layovers or delays.
whoBenefitsMost: Frequent flyers who travel often enough to actually use the lounge visits included with the card.
thingsToRemember: Lounge access usually comes with visit limits and sometimes minimum spend conditions — check the fine print so you know what you're actually entitled to.

title: EMI Flexibility on Big Purchases
description: Large purchases can sometimes be converted into fixed monthly installments (EMIs), spreading the cost over several months instead of paying it all at once.
whoBenefitsMost: People making a planned, necessary large purchase (like an appliance) who want predictable monthly payments instead of one large outflow.
thingsToRemember: EMI conversion often comes with processing fees and interest, even when marketed as 'no-cost' — the cost is sometimes built into the product price instead. Read the terms carefully before converting.

title: Fraud and Purchase Protection
description: Credit cards typically offer stronger fraud protection than cash or debit cards — unauthorized transactions can often be disputed and reversed, and many cards offer purchase protection on eligible items.
whoBenefitsMost: Anyone making online purchases or transactions with unfamiliar merchants, where the risk of fraud or disputes is higher.
thingsToRemember: Protection isn't automatic or unlimited — you usually need to report fraud quickly (often within days) and follow the issuer's dispute process to be covered.

title: Convenience and Spend Tracking
description: Credit cards make it easy to pay without carrying cash, and every transaction is automatically recorded in your statement, making it simpler to track where your money goes.
whoBenefitsMost: Anyone who wants a clearer, automatic record of their spending without maintaining a manual expense log.
thingsToRemember: Convenience can cut both ways — the ease of tapping a card can make it easier to spend without noticing, so it helps to actually review your statement, not just receive it.

title: Interest-Free Period
description: Most credit cards offer a grace period (commonly 20-50 days depending on when you spend in the billing cycle) during which you can repay your balance in full with no interest charged.
whoBenefitsMost: Disciplined spenders who consistently pay their full statement balance by the due date, effectively getting an interest-free short-term loan.
thingsToRemember: This benefit disappears entirely, including on new purchases, the moment you carry forward any unpaid balance — most cards then charge interest from the transaction date, not just on the unpaid portion.

title: Exclusive Offers and Discounts
description: Card issuers often partner with merchants to offer discounts, cashback, or deals on shopping, dining, and travel bookings exclusively for cardholders.
whoBenefitsMost: People who were already planning a purchase and can time it to an existing offer, capturing genuine savings.
thingsToRemember: Don't let a discount talk you into a purchase you weren't already planning to make — a 10% discount doesn't help if it convinces you to spend 100% more than you intended.
